items.details: Most help programs need a score of 620 or higher. A higher score often means you'll pay less to borrow money.

items.task: See how much you've saved for a down payment
items.details: Try to save at least 1% to 3% of the home's price, even if you plan to use a grant. It shows lenders you are ready for this big step.
Lenders look at how much of your monthly income goes to paying debts. Ideally, this should be less than 43% of what you earn before taxes.
Gather your signed Federal tax returns for the last two years.
Collect your W-2 forms from all employers for the last two years.
Save your most recent pay stubs covering at least the last 30 days.
Get the last two months of statements for all your bank and investment accounts.
Make sure you have a valid, unexpired ID, such as a Driver's License or Passport.
Many programs require you to take a class on how to buy a home. You must use an approved housing agency.

glossary.definition: An extra monthly fee you pay if your down payment is less than 20%. It protects the lender, but it adds to your monthly cost.
A safe account where the lender holds some of your monthly payment to pay for your property taxes and home insurance when they are due.
A number your area uses to decide if you qualify for help. If your income is below this, more programs may help you.
A loan where the interest rate never changes. Your monthly payment for the loan itself stays the same for 15 or 30 years.
A loan where the interest rate can go up or down. While it may start lower, your monthly payment can go up a lot later.

glossary.term: Fees for getting your keys (Closing Costs)
glossary.definition: One-time fees paid at the very end when you buy the home. These include things like lawyer fees, taxes, and paperwork.
